01_java_main.title: Java Main and Static Entry

q1.question: What is printed by this snippet?

q2.question: Why is `main` declared `static` here?
options: The JVM needs to call it before any `Main` object exists.
Static methods are always faster than instance methods.
Without `static`, Java cannot print to stdout.
explanations: Correct direction: the entry point must be callable without constructing the class first.
Performance is not the reason the entry point is static.
Printing is unrelated to whether the method is static.
q2.answerReason: The entry point is discovered by the JVM before your program creates application objects.

q3.question: What does `String[] args` represent in `main`?
options: A list of every class loaded by the JVM.
The command-line arguments passed to the program.
The fields declared on the `Main` class.
explanations: Class loading metadata is not passed through `args`.
Yes. `args` is the argument array supplied when the process starts.
Class fields are unrelated to the `main` parameter list.
q3.answerReason: The JVM calls `main` with whatever strings were passed on the command line.

02_kotlin_data_class.title: Kotlin Data Classes
02_kotlin_data_class.description:
Kotlin `data class` generates useful methods for state holders.

```kotlin
data class User(val name: String, val age: Int)
```

The compiler creates `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, and `copy()` from the primary constructor properties.

q2.question: What is the main reason to use `data class` here?
options: It generates value-oriented methods from constructor properties.
It makes the object mutable by default.
It automatically registers the class as a Spring bean.
explanations: Yes. `data class` is about value semantics and compiler-generated helpers.
Mutability comes from `var` versus `val`, not from `data class` itself.
Spring bean registration is separate from Kotlin language features.
q2.answerReason: Use `data class` when the type mainly represents data and equality should depend on its fields.

q3.question: Which generated feature is most useful when you want a modified copy of an immutable object?
options: `hashCode()`
`componentN()`
`copy()`
explanations: `hashCode()` supports hashing and equality-related collections, not cloning with changes.
`componentN()` supports destructuring, not constructing a changed instance.
Correct. `copy()` creates a new instance while letting you override selected properties.
q3.answerReason: `copy()` is what makes small immutable updates ergonomic in Kotlin data models.

03_jvm_memory.title: Stack Frames and Heap Objects
03_jvm_memory.description:
A local reference lives in a stack frame, while the object it points to is typically on the heap.

```java
String name = new String("bean");
```

The reference variable and the object are not the same thing. That distinction matters for GC, aliasing, and mutation through shared references.

q2.question: Why does changing `b.value` affect `a.value`?
options: Both variables refer to the same object instance.
Java silently copies all object fields between variables.
The JVM rewrites both names into one variable during compilation.
explanations: Yes. `a` and `b` are two references to one heap object.
Java does not deep-copy objects on assignment.
Compilation does not merge variable names like that.
q2.answerReason: Assignment of object references copies the reference, not the object itself.

q3.question: What is actually copied by `Box b = a;` in this example?
options: The reference value, not the heap object itself.
A brand-new heap object with all fields duplicated.
Only the `value` field, but not the rest of the object.
explanations: Correct. Both variables end up pointing at the same object instance.
Assignment of object references does not clone the object automatically.
Java does not do partial object copying on simple assignment.
q3.answerReason: Reference assignment aliases one object; it does not construct another object for you.

04_spring_di.title: Constructor Injection

q2.question: Why is constructor injection usually preferred over field injection?
options: It makes required dependencies explicit and easier to test.
It lets Spring skip bean creation until the method is called.
It removes the need for interfaces in Java.
explanations: Correct. Constructor injection is explicit and works well with immutability and tests.
Lazy creation is a different mechanism.
Interfaces are unrelated to field versus constructor injection.
q2.answerReason: If an object cannot work without a dependency, the constructor is the clearest place to require it.

q3.question: Why does constructor injection fit immutable service design well?
options: Dependencies can be required once at construction and stored in final fields.
It allows Spring to ignore missing dependencies safely.
It makes beans run without being registered in the container.
explanations: Yes. Constructor injection pairs naturally with explicit, non-null, fixed dependencies.
Missing required dependencies are still configuration errors.
Spring still needs to create and manage the bean normally.
q3.answerReason: If a service cannot work without a collaborator, the constructor is the cleanest place to enforce that contract.

05_web_controller.title: Controller to Service Flow

q2.question: What is the better responsibility split?
options: Controller handles transport details; service owns business logic.
Controller should contain all SQL because it sees request parameters first.
Service should parse HTTP annotations directly.
explanations: Yes. Keep transport and domain logic separated.
Putting SQL in controllers creates coupling and poor test boundaries.
HTTP annotation parsing belongs to the web layer, not the service layer.
q2.answerReason: Thin controllers scale better because business rules remain reusable outside one endpoint.

q3.question: What usually belongs in the controller rather than the service?
options: The core business rule for calculating totals.
The persistence mapping for a JPA entity.
HTTP-specific concerns like path variables and response status.
explanations: Business rules are usually more reusable when they live in a service layer.
Entity mapping belongs to the persistence model, not the controller.
Correct. Controllers are the transport boundary where HTTP details are handled.
q3.answerReason: Controllers should translate web requests into service calls and translate service results back into HTTP responses.

06_jpa_entity.title: JPA Entity Identity

q1.question: What is printed before persistence assigns an id?

q2.question: Why is entity identity important in JPA?
options: The persistence context tracks one managed instance per database identity.
Entities are just DTOs with extra annotations, so identity does not matter.
Identity only matters for native SQL, not ORM.
explanations: Correct. JPA uses identity to manage change tracking and caching.
Entities have lifecycle and identity semantics beyond DTOs.
Identity is core to ORM behavior too.
q2.answerReason: If two objects claim the same row identity, the persistence context needs a consistent managed instance.

q3.question: What changes when an entity becomes managed by the persistence context?
options: It stops having an identifier.
JPA begins tracking changes on that instance for flush/commit.
It can no longer be compared by identity.
explanations: Managed entities still keep their identity; that is central to tracking them.
Correct. A managed entity is associated with a persistence context that observes updates.
Identity remains important; it does not disappear after persistence begins managing the object.
q3.answerReason: Managed means the ORM is now watching that entity instance as part of the unit of work.

07_transaction_boundary.title: Transaction Boundaries

q2.question: Why is `@Transactional` used around related writes?
options: To keep the unit of work atomic if one step fails.
To make methods public automatically.
To avoid creating any JDBC connections.
explanations: Yes. The goal is atomicity and consistent state.
Visibility is unrelated to transactions.
Transactions do not remove the need for database connections.
q2.answerReason: Atomicity matters when multiple updates must either all commit or all roll back.

q3.question: What failure does a transaction primarily protect against in multi-step writes?
options: Typos in Java variable names.
The JVM loading classes in the wrong order.
A partial commit where early writes escape before a later step fails.
explanations: Transactions do not address source-level typing mistakes.
Class loading is unrelated to transactional integrity.
Correct. The boundary is there so related changes succeed or fail together.
q3.answerReason: Without a transaction, one update may persist even though the whole business action should have failed.

08_spring_test.title: Slice Tests and Fast Feedback

q2.question: Why use a slice test such as `@WebMvcTest`?
options: It narrows the loaded context to the web layer for faster, focused tests.
It automatically tests the real database transaction log.
It replaces assertions with Spring-generated checks.
explanations: Correct. Slice tests reduce scope and speed up feedback.
A web slice test does not load the full database stack by default.
You still write assertions yourself.
q2.answerReason: Focused tests fail for one reason and run quickly enough to stay in the normal workflow.

q3.question: Why are focused tests valuable even when you also have full integration tests?
options: They replace the need for integration tests completely.
They isolate one layer and fail faster with a narrower cause.
They guarantee every Spring bean is loaded.
explanations: Focused tests help, but they do not eliminate the need for broader coverage.
Correct. Narrow scope keeps feedback quick and the reason for failure easier to see.
A slice test intentionally does not load every bean.
q3.answerReason: Good test pyramids use faster, narrower tests to catch many issues before the full application needs to start.
